title: Off Camera

jokes.text: We're an hour away From show 1,457, Not that We keep count.
jokes.setup: Claims they don't keep count of shows
jokes.punchline: While immediately giving the exact show number
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan contradiction - Artie's gruff pragmatism comes through in claiming they don't obsess while proving they obsess. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: We're an hour away From show 1,457, Not that We keep count.

jokes.text: You think we've Busted our balls Doing 1,400 shows So Larry can get laid? If mr. Sanders Wanted to fuck talent, He'd have been An agent.
jokes.setup: Defending Larry against romantic implications
jokes.punchline: Brutal industry burn about agents
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from defensive to savage industry critique. Perfect character voice - Artie's cynical wisdom about Hollywood hierarchy. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You think we've Busted our balls Doing 1,400 shows So Larry can get laid? If mr. Sanders Wanted to fuck talent, He'd have been An agent.

jokes.text: Put the nursing home group In the upper left corner. That's the studio's Warmest place.
jokes.setup: Artie arranging audience seating
jokes.punchline: Pragmatic concern for elderly comfort reveals unexpected thoughtfulness
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment - shows Artie's unexpected consideration beneath gruff exterior.

dialogue.speaker: Artie

jokes.text: I can't. I-- i-- I'm married, liz. Oh, how is The little woman? Huh? Your wife?
jokes.setup: Artie trying to resist Elizabeth's advances
jokes.punchline: Elizabeth's condescending 'little woman' comment
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe - the awkwardness of workplace seduction mixed with dismissive attitude toward marriage.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I can't. I-- i-- I'm married, liz.

dialogue.speaker: Elizabeth Ashley
dialogue.line: Oh, how is The little woman? Huh? Your wife?

jokes.text: What do you think Of a woman Who'd leave her home For three months To photograph zebras Humping? I think that Daddy's lonesome, too.
jokes.setup: Artie complaining about his wife's safari photography
jokes.punchline: Elizabeth's seductive 'daddy's lonesome' response
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Artie's crude complaint gets turned into seduction opportunity. 'Zebras humping' is a funny specific detail.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: What do you think Of a woman Who'd leave her home For three months To photograph zebras Humping?

dialogue.speaker: Elizabeth Ashley
dialogue.line: I think that Daddy's lonesome, too.

jokes.text: We're leaning On Larry's suits. Do I look like I give a shit? Jump on back here, Little big man. Ever since you've Gone on slim-fast, I'm powerless.
jokes.setup: Someone warns they're messing up Larry's wardrobe
jokes.punchline: Artie's dismissal followed by Elizabeth's Slim-Fast comment
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup with diet product reference. 'Little big man' is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Elizabeth Ashley
dialogue.line: We're leaning On Larry's suits.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Do I look like I give a shit? Jump on back here, Little big man.

dialogue.speaker: Elizabeth Ashley
dialogue.line: Ever since you've Gone on slim-fast, I'm powerless.

jokes.text: Are you In your thoughts? No. I'm just Going over The cue cards.
jokes.setup: Hank using pretentious 'in your thoughts' phrase
jokes.punchline: Larry's mundane, practical response
jokes.explanation: Perfect character dynamics - Hank's affected showbiz speak vs Larry's practical reality.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Are you In your thoughts?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No. I'm just Going over The cue cards.

jokes.text: No, if you had a bug, I'd say, 'go like this.' Some hair's Sticking up. Oh, o.K. Nothing major.
jokes.setup: Larry gestures at his hair
jokes.punchline: The logical explanation of bug vs. hair protocol
jokes.explanation: Nice observational humor about backstage grooming protocols.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, if you had a bug, I'd say, 'go like this.' Some hair's Sticking up. Oh, o.K. Nothing major.

jokes.text: No shit. Who's this key chain?
jokes.setup: Meeting John Ritter
jokes.punchline: Dismissive reference to Ritter's agent as 'key chain'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Artie-ism - reducing an agent to a trivial accessory. Perfect character voice and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: No shit. Who's this key chain?

jokes.text: I've never even seen hearts afire. Now, I'm involved in some Conversation about retooling-- Make a note.
jokes.setup: Larry forced into small talk about Ritter's show
jokes.punchline: His irritation at being drawn into industry chat
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Larry's neurotic need to control interactions vs. polite social obligations.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I've never even seen hearts afire. Now, I'm involved in some Conversation about retooling-- Make a note.

jokes.text: If it wasn't for me, This dog would be dead now. Look at this little face. This face Would be dead. Yeah, well, that Would be a tragedy. What would his fleas do?
jokes.setup: Darlene's emotional plea about saving the dog
jokes.punchline: Artie's deadpan concern for the fleas' welfare
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion - takes emotional animal rescue story and focuses on parasites. Perfect Artie cynicism with unexpected logic.

dialogue.speaker: Darlene
dialogue.line: If it wasn't for me, This dog would be dead now. Look at this little face. This face Would be dead.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, that Would be a tragedy. What would his fleas do?

jokes.text: Look at his neck. He's a flea hotel.
jokes.setup: Continuing criticism of the flea-infested dog
jokes.punchline: Metaphor comparing dog's neck to hospitality business
jokes.explanation: Nice wordplay - 'flea hotel' is both descriptive and metaphorically clever.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Look at his neck. He's a flea hotel.

jokes.text: I'm a little slow, Brain's broccoli, But, hey, no problem.
jokes.setup: Elizabeth explaining she's feeling sluggish
jokes.punchline: Creative vegetable metaphor for mental state
jokes.explanation: 'Brain's broccoli' is a creative way to describe mental fog.

dialogue.speaker: Elizabeth Ashley
dialogue.line: I'm a little slow, Brain's broccoli, But, hey, no problem.

jokes.text: Because my hands, Like my brains, Left to their Own devices, Get away from me And flap. Southern ladies Always hold something. Also, you never know When you'll get The vapors and faint.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about her fan
jokes.punchline: Elaborate explanation mixing practical and stereotypical Southern behavior
jokes.explanation: Good character work - mixing genuine nervousness with Southern belle affectation.

dialogue.speaker: Elizabeth Ashley
dialogue.line: Because my hands, Like my brains, Left to their Own devices, Get away from me And flap. Southern ladies Always hold something. Also, you never know When you'll get The vapors and faint.

jokes.text: Not really. Oh, that Blake edwards movie. What was that? skin deep. You know that Glow-in-the-dark Condom scene?
jokes.setup: Ritter asking if Siskel said good things about his work
jokes.punchline: Siskel's blunt 'not really' followed by specific embarrassing scene reference
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - critic's brutal honesty with specific embarrassing detail.

dialogue.speaker: Gene Siskel
dialogue.line: Not really.

dialogue.speaker: Gene Siskel
dialogue.line: Oh, that Blake edwards movie. What was that? skin deep. You know that Glow-in-the-dark Condom scene?

jokes.text: I know you're Basically a TV actor, But surely you know The difference Between a good movie And a bad movie, Having made both problem child 1 and 2.
jokes.setup: Siskel condescendingly addressing Ritter's film knowledge
jokes.punchline: Brutal reminder of Ritter's bad movie track record
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - starts patronizing then delivers knockout punch with specific bad movies.

dialogue.speaker: Gene Siskel
dialogue.line: I know you're Basically a TV actor, But surely you know The difference Between a good movie And a bad movie, Having made both problem child 1 and 2.

jokes.text: How long ago was that? Five years ago? You remember it pretty well For an actor Who doesn't listen To critics.
jokes.setup: Ritter claiming he doesn't care about critics
jokes.punchline: Siskel pointing out the contradiction in remembering the exact words
jokes.explanation: Smart logical trap - catches the contradiction between claiming indifference and demonstrating detailed memory.

dialogue.speaker: Gene Siskel
dialogue.line: How long ago was that? Five years ago? You remember it pretty well For an actor Who doesn't listen To critics.

jokes.text: I mean, listen, I think you are a sugar pop, You are just cute as a button, But I need something Up here to grab ahold of. Right. Maybe we could Weld a handle Or something Up there.
jokes.setup: Elizabeth rejecting Hank for lacking intelligence
jokes.punchline: Hank's literal interpretation about welding a handle to his head
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - completely missing the metaphor and suggesting literal solution. Great character-specific stupidity.

dialogue.speaker: Elizabeth Ashley
dialogue.line: I mean, listen, I think you are a sugar pop, You are just cute as a button, But I need something Up here to grab ahold of.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Right. Maybe we could Weld a handle Or something Up there.

jokes.text: This fucking maniac Attacked my client. These fucking people From chicago-- Hey-- This is all they know-- Punching and fists. Hey, cut the chicago crap. All you people from l.A. Know Is how to validate your parking.
jokes.setup: Agent complaining about Chicago people being violent
jokes.punchline: Siskel's comeback about LA people only knowing parking validation
jokes.explanation: Great regional stereotype battle - Chicago violence vs LA superficiality. Sharp, quotable comeback.

dialogue.speaker: Adam Loderman
dialogue.line: This fucking maniac Attacked my client. These fucking people From chicago--

dialogue.speaker: Gene Siskel
dialogue.line: Hey-- This is all they know-- Punching and fists. Hey, cut the chicago crap. All you people from l.A. Know Is how to validate your parking.

jokes.text: It's bad being On the show With him, But then I get assaulted By his fucking dog! I don't have A dog. Did he bite you in the face? No, he got between my legs, I tripped, I hit My face on the table.
jokes.setup: Agent complaining about dog attack
jokes.punchline: Reveals he wasn't actually bitten, just tripped over the dog
jokes.explanation: Decent physical comedy deflation - builds up dog attack then reveals it was just clumsiness.

dialogue.speaker: Adam Loderman
dialogue.line: It's bad being On the show With him, But then I get assaulted By his fucking dog!

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I don't have A dog.
Did he bite you in the face?

dialogue.speaker: Adam Loderman
dialogue.line: No, he got between my legs, I tripped, I hit My face on the table.

jokes.text: I bet this shit doesn't even Happen on montel williams.
jokes.setup: Siskel dealing with backstage chaos
jokes.punchline: Comparison to daytime talk show as preferable alternative
jokes.explanation: Good reference humor - comparing prestigious late night to chaotic daytime TV.

dialogue.speaker: Gene Siskel
dialogue.line: I bet this shit doesn't even Happen on montel williams.

jokes.text: If you don't Shut the fuck up, I will kick you In the nuts so hard, Your dentist Will have to Work around them At your next cleaning. Ha! Just kidding.
jokes.setup: Artie building to violent threat
jokes.punchline: Elaborate, specific threat followed by casual 'just kidding'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation with vivid, absurd imagery followed by perfect deflation. The dentist detail makes it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If you don't Shut the fuck up, I will kick you In the nuts so hard, Your dentist Will have to Work around them At your next cleaning. Ha! Just kidding.

dialogue.line: Uh, no, john ritter Will be batting clean-up. By then the bases Will be loaded.

dialogue.line: Because Were I dating, I would look In the paper And take a woman To see free willy, And I'd nudge her When he comes heaving Out of the water-- See if she Gets the hint.

jokes.text: You're kidding. No. No. You're not kidding. Hank, stephen rea's Character... The woman he falls In love with. That dark-skinned cutie? Mm-hmm. She turns out To be a man. I don't think so.
jokes.setup: Siskel explaining The Crying Game plot twist
jokes.punchline: Hank's complete denial of obvious plot point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - his refusal to accept reality even when explicitly explained. Great character-specific obliviousness.

jokes.punchline: Hank still in denial despite anatomical evidence

jokes.punchline: Hank's innocent question with unintended double meaning

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: How was she hung?

jokes.text: It's been years Since he's seen A movie Without The words 'busty' Or 'stewardess' In the title.

jokes.punchline: Specific description of Hank's lowbrow movie preferences
jokes.explanation: Good character assassination - specific details about Hank's terrible taste in movies.

dialogue.speaker: Larry

jokes.text: Adam, they Fucking bumped me. Adam! Hey, buddy. Don't you talk to me. We're just trying To give you more time. Artie. What?

jokes.punchline: Agent's weak excuse and Ritter's justified anger

dialogue.speaker: Adam Loderman
dialogue.line: Hey, buddy. Don't you talk to me. We're just trying To give you more time.

jokes.text: This is the last time I'm ever doing this show! The last time!
jokes.setup: Ritter's mounting frustration with the evening
jokes.punchline: Complete breakdown and threat to never return
jokes.explanation: Predictable guest meltdown - familiar territory but serves the episode's chaos theme.

dialogue.speaker: John Ritter
dialogue.line: This is the last time I'm ever doing this show! The last time!

jokes.text: I think I speak for everyone When I say I can't believe We get paid To do this.
jokes.setup: Reporter asking how the show went
jokes.punchline: Larry's oblivious assessment after a disaster show
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Larry's complete disconnect from reality. The line works on multiple levels.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I think I speak for everyone When I say I can't believe We get paid To do this.

jokes.text: I've never understood Why you read this shit. Because it's an article About the show. Well, I'm sure it's Excellent publicity.
jokes.setup: Artie dismissing entertainment journalism
jokes.punchline: Larry's naive optimism about press coverage
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Artie's cynicism vs Larry's desperate need for positive press.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I've never understood Why you read this shit.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Because it's an article About the show.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure it's Excellent publicity.

jokes.text: This guy says ritter And gene siskel fought. A dog bit phil. You and phil Were wrestling. It says you And elizabeth ashley Were doing God-knows-what In the wardrobe Room.
jokes.setup: Larry reading the disastrous press coverage
jokes.punchline: Catalog of all the evening's mishaps as reported
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure - summarizes the evening's chaos through Larry's shocked reading.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This guy says ritter And gene siskel fought. A dog bit phil. You and phil Were wrestling. It says you And elizabeth ashley Were doing God-knows-what In the wardrobe Room.

jokes.text: They come on a show, it's not Exciting enough for them, They make up this shit.
jokes.setup: Artie defending against negative press coverage
jokes.punchline: Claims the chaos was fabricated when we saw it all happen
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-joke - Artie claims events were fabricated when audience saw they were absolutely true. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: They come on a show, it's not Exciting enough for them, They make up this shit.

tmdb_overview: Artie has a terrible day backstage with an Entertainment Weekly writer prowling around, John Ritter and Gene Siskel nearly coming to blows, and a stray dog nipping at everyone.
